entries.name: Matrix (Synapse + Element)
entries.what: Federated chat you host yourself — the UK-governed open standard behind Element, used by governments and public bodies across Europe.
entries.note: Synapse/Element are AGPL with a CLA enabling Element's commercial dual-licensing — open-core at the edges. A busy homeserver needs real admin attention.

entries.name: Signal
entries.what: The benchmark for encrypted messaging — but a centralised service run from the US, so it is context here, not sovereign infrastructure.
entries.note: Code is open but there is no federation; self-hosting is impractical, so you depend on the Signal Foundation's US-run infrastructure.

entries.name: SimpleX Chat
entries.what: Encrypted messaging with no user IDs at all, built by a London company; communities can run their own relay servers.
entries.note: Active (v6.5 stable, v7.0 beta, mid-2026) and independently audited, but a small team; groups and desktop are rougher than Signal.

entries.name: Briar
entries.what: Peer-to-peer messaging over Tor, Wi-Fi or Bluetooth — no servers to seize, and it keeps working when the internet goes down.
entries.note: Android-first (desktop still limited); serverless by design — an optional Mailbox app on a spare phone handles offline delivery. Slow, deliberate release pace (1.5.17, March 2026).

entries.name: Delta Chat
entries.what: Chat that rides on ordinary email — any mailbox works, or run a chatmail relay for your community on a small server.
entries.note: Publicly funded (EU/NLnet); 2026 releases cut server-visible metadata to near zero and added native calls. Niche user base compared with Signal or Matrix.

entries.name: XMPP (Prosody / ejabberd)
entries.what: The veteran open messaging standard; Prosody runs a community chat server on the smallest of machines.
entries.note: Both servers actively maintained through 2026. Client quality varies — for a polished out-of-box experience pair with a curated distribution such as Snikket.

entries.name: Meshtastic
entries.what: Off-grid text messaging over cheap LoRa radios — neighbourhood mesh networks with no SIM, no internet and no radio licence.
entries.note: Runs licence-free on 868 MHz in the UK. Delivery is best-effort and text-only — brilliant for resilience and events, not a Slack replacement.

entries.name: Jitsi Meet
entries.what: Video calls in the browser with no accounts needed; self-host the whole stack on your own server.
entries.note: Stewarded by US firm 8x8, but fully open and self-hostable so there is no service dependency. End-to-end encryption only works reliably in smaller calls.

entries.name: BigBlueButton
entries.what: A virtual classroom you run yourself — whiteboard, breakout rooms, polls and recordings, widely used in education.
entries.note: Built for teaching rather than quick meetings; wants a dedicated Ubuntu server with decent specs. Steward Blindside Networks; 3.0.x actively maintained (July 2026).

entries.name: Logos Messaging
entries.what: Peer-to-peer messaging for apps that want no servers at all — the transport underneath the Status app.
entries.note: Formerly Waku. nwaku still 0.x (v0.38, May 2026); Status runs on it, but the incentivised public network remains research/testnet-stage — not production for third parties.

entries.name: Radar Chat
entries.what: Encrypted messaging with Bitcoin built in — send sats inside the conversation, keys stay yours.
entries.note: Launched July 2026 by the Cake Wallet team, which financially supports Signal. The app's own source was not verifiably published at listing time — FOSS status unverified.

entries.name: Tor
entries.what: The anonymity network. Publish onion services from hardware you control and reach users no platform can block.
entries.note: Steward is a US non-profit but relays and onion services run anywhere; actively released (Browser 15.0.17, June 2026) and the Rust rewrite Arti is maturing fast.

entries.name: libp2p
entries.what: The networking library under Ethereum, IPFS and Polkadot — peer discovery, transports and encryption for building your own p2p apps.
entries.origin: US origin (Protocol Labs), now global community
entries.note: Battle-tested across major blockchain and IPFS deployments, with Go, Rust, JS and other implementations all releasing in 2026; US-rooted stewardship is the main caveat.

entries.name: I2P
entries.what: Anonymous overlay network built for hosting services inside the network itself; smaller than Tor but fully self-contained.
entries.license: Public domain / mixed; i2pd is BSD-3-Clause
entries.note: Alive and active in 2026 (Java I2P 2.12, C++ i2pd 2.60) with post-quantum crypto landing; much smaller anonymity set than Tor and slower to join.

entries.name: Yggdrasil
entries.what: Self-arranging encrypted IPv6 mesh: gives every machine a stable address that works across NATs, VPSes and ad-hoc links.
entries.note: Reliable in daily use and released as recently as February 2026 (0.5.13), but self-describes as an experimental hobbyist project; routing protocol still evolves between versions.

entries.name: Reticulum
entries.what: Networking stack for off-grid and unreliable links — LoRa, packet radio, serial, WiFi — with ready-made encrypted messaging apps on top.
entries.license: Reticulum License (non-OSI, was MIT); MIT community fork available
entries.note: Not strictly FOSS since April 2025: custom licence bans AI-training and weapons use. MIT-licensed community fork (Reticulum_CE) exists; founder is stepping back.

entries.name: Nym
entries.what: A live mixnet that hides traffic patterns, not just content. Use it via NymVPN today or as a transport for your own apps.
entries.note: All code is FOSS and actively shipped (binaries v2026.13, July 2026), but running mixnet nodes requires bonding NYM tokens and NymVPN is a paid service.

entries.name: GNUnet
entries.what: GNU's peer-to-peer framework, home of the GNS name system — a censorship-resistant DNS replacement — and plumbing shared with GNU Taler.
entries.note: Actively released (0.27, March 2026) and GNS is standardised as RFC 9498, but the project itself says it suits early adopters with 'reasonable pain tolerance'.

entries.name: Veilid
entries.what: Framework for building private p2p apps — DHT, encrypted routing, mobile and browser support — from the Cult of the Dead Cow.
entries.note: Actively developed (veilid-core 0.5.3, March 2026) but almost nothing ships on it in production yet; a promising research bet, not a dependency.

entries.name: Syncthing
entries.what: Keeps folders in sync directly between your own devices, peer to peer — no cloud account, no central server, nothing to rent.
entries.note: Community foundation, donation-funded, over a decade old; v2 line shipped 2025 and releases are steady. The quiet workhorse of this list.

entries.name: Nextcloud
entries.what: Self-hosted files, calendars, contacts, documents and video calls — the standard replacement for Google Workspace or Microsoft 365.
entries.note: All code AGPL; the paid tier is support, not withheld features. Widely deployed by European governments. Large installs need real admin effort.

entries.name: IPFS (Kubo)
entries.what: Content-addressed peer-to-peer file network: files are fetched by what they are, not where they live, from whoever holds a copy.
entries.note: Mature and actively released (v0.42, 2026), but files persist only while someone pins them — plan your own pinning. US-stewarded.

entries.name: Yjs
entries.what: The building block behind most collaborative editors — adds Google-Docs-style live editing and offline merging to your own app.
entries.note: The most deployed CRDT library in the world; huge ecosystem of editor bindings. A developer library, not an end-user product.

entries.name: Automerge
entries.what: A toolkit for apps that work offline on each device and merge everyone's changes automatically when they reconnect.
entries.origin: US (Ink & Switch) with Cambridge (UK) research roots
entries.note: Version 3.0 (Aug 2025) fixed the old memory and speed problems; production footprint still younger and smaller than Yjs.

entries.name: ElectricSQL
entries.what: Syncs your Postgres data down into local apps so they load instantly, work offline and update live — you keep the database.
entries.note: 1.0 GA March 2025 with named production users; sync engine fully open. Company is rebranding towards AI-agent sync — watch the direction.

entries.name: Garage
entries.what: S3-compatible object storage you can run across a few second-hand servers in different buildings and still survive an outage.
entries.note: Run in production by its non-profit steward since 2020; stable release April 2026. Built for small clusters, not petabytes.

entries.name: Tahoe-LAFS
entries.what: Encrypted, erasure-coded storage spread across servers that never see your data — a grandparent of the decentralised storage field.
entries.note: Cryptographically solid and stable, but slow-moving: last release December 2024, small community. Expect to invest in operating it yourself.

entries.name: Logos Storage
entries.what: Decentralised storage with durability guarantees — aims to keep files provably alive without trusting any single provider.
entries.note: Formerly Codex. Public testnet paused Sept 2025 for a redesign, mainnet targeted 2027; code active (v0.4.1, July 2026).

entries.name: Logos Basecamp
entries.what: Desktop app bundling the Logos stack — wallet, node, storage, chat — running entirely on your own machine, no hosted backend.
entries.note: v0.2.1 with Linux and macOS installers; open source. The app runs entirely on your device — the bundled modules currently participate in the Logos testnet (mainnet targeted 2027).

entries.name: CryptPad
entries.what: Encrypted collaborative docs, sheets, kanban and diagrams — even the server cannot read them. Runs happily on a small VPS.
entries.note: Built by XWiki SAS, partly EU-grant funded; ships in Germany's openDesk public-sector suite. End-to-end encryption means no server-side search or content recovery. Active: 2026.5.1 released May 2026.

entries.name: Collabora Online
entries.what: LibreOffice in the browser: shared editing of documents and spreadsheets on your own server. Built in Cambridge.
entries.note: Fully MPL-2.0 — free CODE builds for self-hosters; the company sells support, not features. One of the few genuinely UK-stewarded options in this category. Powers Nextcloud Office.

entries.name: OnlyOffice (and the Euro-Office fork)
entries.what: Web office suite with the best Microsoft-format fidelity going; plugs into Nextcloud and self-hosts cleanly.
entries.note: Development reportedly Russia-based; a European consortium (Nextcloud, IONOS, Proton, XWiki) forked it as Euro-Office in 2026 after a licence dispute — mature code, very young fork.

entries.name: Forgejo
entries.what: Code hosting without GitHub — repos, issues, pull requests and CI on a server you control. Powers Codeberg.
entries.note: Copyleft since v9 so it cannot go proprietary; v15.0.3 shipped June 2026. Nonprofit Codeberg e.V. offers hosted repos free if you would rather not run your own.

entries.name: Penpot
entries.what: Design and prototyping tool teams actually switch to from Figma — self-hosted with one Docker Compose file.
entries.note: Built by Kaleidos (Madrid); 2.16.2 released July 2026. New WebGL renderer is still beta for very large files, but the switch from Figma is realistic today.

entries.name: Discourse
entries.what: The default choice for community forums — runs your discussion space on your own box with mature moderation tools.
entries.note: US-company steward, but the GPL code is fully self-hostable and the firm publicly recommitted to open source in April 2026. Official plugins are also open.

entries.name: Mastodon (and the Fediverse)
entries.what: Run your own social network that talks to millions across the Fediverse — your instance, your rules, your data.
entries.note: Steward restructuring under a Belgian nonprofit parent in 2026. UK instances exist and EU managed hosts (e.g. masto.host, Portugal) will run one for a small community cheaply.

entries.name: Decidim
entries.what: Participatory democracy platform for consultations, budgets and assemblies — built by cities, proven at city scale.
entries.note: Runs Barcelona's and EU institutions' participation; steward is the Barcelona-based Decidim Free Software Association. A heavyweight Rails app — budget real sysadmin time or hire a Decidim provider.

entries.name: Loomio
entries.what: Discussion and decision-making for groups: proposals, votes and a clear record of what was agreed, without meetings.
entries.note: Fourteen years old, run by a small worker co-op. Official Docker setup makes self-hosting practical, but expect to be your own support; hosted service exists.

entries.name: Open Collective
entries.what: Transparent money management for communities — raise funds and pay expenses in the open under a fiscal host.
entries.note: The hosted platform is the product: the code is MIT but not practically self-hostable. Community-governed (OFiCo) since its exit-to-community; Brussels-based Open Collective Europe hosts EU groups.

entries.name: PeerTube
entries.what: Your own video platform, federated so viewers find you across instances — no YouTube dependence required.
entries.note: Stewarded by nonprofit Framasoft; v8.2.2 shipped July 2026. Peer-to-peer streaming keeps bandwidth bills sane for small hosts; video transcoding still wants a beefy server.

entries.name: Hetzner
entries.what: German dedicated servers and cloud VMs at famously low prices; the default budget choice for EU-hosted startups.
entries.note: Not FOSS — a provider. GPU range is single-card boxes (up to RTX Pro 6000, 96GB): fine for inference, not cluster training.

entries.name: OVHcloud
entries.what: France's biggest cloud: bare metal, managed Kubernetes and H100/H200 GPU instances, all under EU jurisdiction.
entries.note: Not FOSS. Widest EU GPU catalogue and outside CLOUD Act reach, but a fraction of AWS/Azure scale; frontier-scale training still means US clouds.

entries.name: Scaleway
entries.what: Developer-friendly French cloud with H100 GPU instances and EU data residency; the closest European feel to a US hyperscaler.
entries.note: Not FOSS. H100s rentable today, typically 5-6x cheaper than hyperscaler EU regions — but capacity and product breadth remain far smaller.

entries.name: Mythic Beasts
entries.what: Small Cambridge ISP loved by UK developers: VPS, dedicated servers and a Raspberry Pi cloud, run by engineers who answer email.
entries.note: Not FOSS. No GPUs — this is where you host your app in the UK, not where you train models.

entries.name: Webarchitects
entries.what: Sheffield hosting co-operative: managed Nextcloud, WordPress, email and VPS on green power — and you can join as a member-owner.
entries.note: A service, not software, but everything they run is open source. Small by design — suits communities and NGOs more than high-growth startups.

entries.name: Co-op Cloud
entries.what: Recipe-based deployment for community software — Nextcloud, Outline, Matrix — on any cheap VPS, built and used by tech co-ops.
entries.note: Still self-describes as beta (abra 0.13.0-beta, Feb 2026) but actively maintained and used by co-ops hosting paying clients.

entries.name: Mistral open-weight models
entries.what: French lab whose Mistral 3 family — 3B up to a 675B mixture-of-experts — ships under Apache 2.0 and runs on your own hardware.
entries.note: Current generation genuinely Apache 2.0; some earlier releases used a restrictive research licence, so check per model. Company monetises via hosted API.

entries.name: Qwen
entries.what: Alibaba's open-weight family, 0.6B to 235B+; arguably the strongest Apache 2.0 models you can download and run offline today.
entries.note: Chinese steward, so not European in origin — but the weights are Apache 2.0 and run entirely on your own hardware, no phone-home.

entries.name: EuroLLM
entries.what: EU-funded multilingual models (9B and 22B, Apache 2.0) trained in Europe to cover every official EU language.
entries.note: Downloadable today but well behind Mistral and Qwen on capability; sibling project OpenEuroLLM expects first models summer 2026. Watch, don't depend.

entries.name: llama.cpp
entries.what: The C++ engine that runs open-weight models on laptops, Macs and Raspberry Pis; most local AI tools are built on top of it.
entries.note: Extremely active, near-daily releases. Also runs Meta's Llama weights — though Meta's own model licence is not OSI-approved.

entries.name: Ollama
entries.what: One-command local model runner: type 'ollama run mistral' and you are chatting offline on your own machine.
entries.note: Open-core drift: CLI and server are MIT, but the 2025 desktop app is closed-source and a cloud upsell has appeared. Fine locally; watch the trajectory.

entries.name: vLLM
entries.what: The standard open server for GPU inference at scale: OpenAI-compatible API and high throughput on hardware you control.
entries.note: US-origin but vendor-neutral under the PyTorch Foundation; what you run on rented EU GPUs when you outgrow a laptop.

entries.name: Sparrow Wallet
entries.what: Desktop Bitcoin wallet for holding your own keys — multisig, hardware-wallet support, and it connects to your own node.
entries.note: Self-custody itself isn't an FCA-regulated activity; promoting or dealing cryptoassets to UK customers is. Solo-maintained but six years of steady releases (v2.5, May 2026).

entries.name: Safe
entries.what: Multisig accounts on Ethereum: a shared team treasury where moving funds needs several signatures, not one person's laptop.
entries.note: Heavily audited contracts securing tens of billions on-chain. Most teams use the hosted interface; self-hosting the full open-source stack is real work.

entries.name: Bitcoin (Bitcoin Core)
entries.what: The original settlement layer — slow and costly per transaction, but extraordinarily hard to censor, seize or quietly change.
entries.note: Best treated as an anchoring and final-settlement layer; ten-minute blocks and price volatility make it a poor everyday retail rail.

entries.name: GNU Taler
entries.what: Digital cash with payer privacy: customers spend anonymously, merchants stay visible and taxable. Ordinary servers, no blockchain.
entries.note: v1.0 shipped May 2025; Swiss-franc operator live but merchant acceptance still tiny. No UK operator — realistic today for self-run closed systems (vouchers, local schemes).

entries.name: Komunitin
entries.what: Wallet and marketplace software for mutual-credit community currencies — LETS, time banks — including trade between communities.
entries.note: Successor to IntegralCES, used daily by Spanish exchange networks; tiny team but active (commits July 2026). Cyclos, the household name here, went proprietary in 2012.

entries.name: walt.id
entries.what: Toolkit for issuing and verifying digital credentials to eIDAS 2.0/EUDI wallet standards — identity rails without a US vendor.
entries.note: Open-core: the Apache-2.0 community stack works but is still 0.x (v0.22, July 2026); persistence, multi-tenancy and support sit in the paid enterprise tier.

entries.name: ZKPassport
entries.what: Prove you are over 18, a given nationality or a real human from your passport's chip — without showing the passport itself.
entries.note: London-built, now stewarded by Aztec Labs with code kept open. Real deployments (17,000+ verifications) but young; proof generation runs through their mobile app.

entries.name: Logos Consensus
entries.what: Privacy-preserving proof-of-stake blockchain, intended as the settlement layer for self-governing communities.
entries.note: Formerly Nomos. Public testnet (v0.2, June 2026, ~350 nodes), mainnet targeted 2027. Run a node if curious.

entries.name: Cake Wallet
entries.what: Self-custody wallet for Bitcoin, Monero and more — keys on your device, and you can connect your own node.
entries.note: Open source since 2018 with active releases; Monero heritage, now multi-currency across Android, iOS, macOS and Linux.

entries.name: Monero
entries.what: Digital cash that is private by default — payments untraceable on-chain, run your own node, no company behind it.
entries.note: Community-run since 2014, releasing steadily (v0.18.5.1, July 2026). UK reality check: FCA-supervised exchanges have largely delisted privacy coins, so acquiring it here means peer-to-peer or decentralised routes.
